coredata.dc:title: Biological and catalytic applications of green synthesized fluorescent N-doped carbon dots using Hylocereus undatus
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Arul, Velusamy
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Lee, Yong Rok
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple hydrothermal route for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is reported. The Hylocereus undatus (H. undatus) extract and aqueous ammonia are used as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs are analyzed using UV–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The surface morphology, elemental composition, crystallinity and functional groups present in the N-CDs are examined using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs emit strong blue fluorescence at 400nm under the excitation of 320nm. Further, the excitation dependent emission properties are also observed from the fluorescence of synthesized N-CDs. The HR-TEM results reveal that synthesized N-CDs are in spherical shape with average diameter of 2.5nm. The XRD pattern exhibits, the graphitic nature of synthesized N-CDs. The doping of nitrogen is confirmed from the EDS and FT-IR studies. The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of N-CDs are evaluated through MTT assay on L-929 (Lymphoblastoid-929) and MCF-7 (Michigan Cancer Foundation-7) cells. The results indicate that the fluorescent N-CDs show less cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility on both L-929 and MCF-7 cells. Moreover, the N-CDs show excellent catalytic activity towards the reduction of methylene blue by sodium borohydride.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hylocereus undatus
N-doped carbon dots
Hydrothermal carbonization
Catalytic activity
Cytotoxicity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of H. undatus Fruit Extract
Evaluation of the Catalytic Activity of Synthesized N-CDs
Catalytic Activity of N-CDs in the Reduction of Methylene Blue Using NaBH4
Evaluation of In-vitro Cytotoxicity Using MTT Assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–Vis spectra of H. undatus extract and synthesized N-CDs; b) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of synthesized N-CDs.

a) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 275nm to 320nm; b) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 320nm to 380nm.
a) HR-TEM images of the prepared N-CDs at 20nm scale, b) Size distribution chart for N-CDs, c) High resolution image of prepared N-CDs at 2nm scale, d) SAED pattern of N-CDs.
EDS spectrum of the prepared N-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of H. undatus Extract and prepared N-CDs.
XRD pattern of the synthesized N-CDs.
(a) UV–Vis spectra of the reduction of MB using NaBH4 in the absence of N-CDs; (b) UV–Vis spectra of the reduction of MB using NaBH4 in the presence of N-CDs.
Microscopic images of L-929 cells after incubation of 24h with various concentrations of N-CDs a) 0μL/mL, b) 10μL/mL, c) 20μL/mL, d) 30μL/mL, e) 40μL/mL and f) 50μL/mL using MTT assay.
Microscopic images of MCF-7 cells after incubation of 24h with various concentrations of N-CDs a) 0μL/mL b) 10μL/mL c) 20μL/mL d) 30μL/mL e) 40μL/mL and f) 50μL/mL using MTT assay.
Cell viability (%) of L-929 cells after incubation of 24h with various concentrations of synthesized N-CDs.
Cell viability (%) of MCF-7 cells after incubation of 24h with various concentrations of synthesized N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Biological and catalytic applications of green synthesized fluorescent N-doped carbon dots using Hylocereus undatus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed University, Gandhigram 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple hydrothermal route for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is reported. The Hylocereus undatus (H. undatus) extract and aqueous ammonia are used as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs are analyzed using UV–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The surface morphology, elemental composition, crystallinity and functional groups present in the N-CDs are examined using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs emit strong blue fluorescence at 400nm under the excitation of 320nm. Further, the excitation dependent emission properties are also observed from the fluorescence of synthesized N-CDs. The HR-TEM results reveal that synthesized N-CDs are in spherical shape with average diameter of 2.5nm. The XRD pattern exhibits, the graphitic nature of synthesized N-CDs. The doping of nitrogen is confirmed from the EDS and FT-IR studies. The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of N-CDs are evaluated through MTT assay on L-929 (Lymphoblastoid-929) and MCF-7 (Michigan Cancer Foundation-7) cells. The results indicate that the fluorescent N-CDs show less cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility on both L-929 and MCF-7 cells. Moreover, the N-CDs show excellent catalytic activity towards the reduction of methylene blue by sodium borohydride.

ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots (CDs) are new class of carbon materials with size ranging below 10nm and they are accidently discovered during the purification of single wall nanotubes (SWNT's) in 2004 [1–3]. The CDs attract many researchers due to their fascinating properties such as excellent water solubility, high photo stability, unique excitation-dependent fluorescence, low toxicity and superior biocompatibility [4–6]. Owing to the unique properties, CDs are applied in the fields of bioimaging, sensing, catalysis, and light emitting devices [7–10]. Several physical, chemical and electrochemical methods have been employed for the synthesis of CDs such as laser ablation of graphite, hydrothermal and solvo-thermal carbonization of organic compounds, microwave pyrolysis and ultra-sonication [11,12]. Among these, hydrothermal carbonization method is simple, eco-friendly, safe and cost effective for the synthesis of CDs. Many carbon precursors including carbohydrates, ascorbic acid, citric acid, tannic acid and organic sources have been utilized for the synthesis of CDs through the hydrothermal method. The synthesis of CDs mainly depends on acidic, basic and neutral constituents present in the plant extracts. The most favorable condition for preparing high yield CDs is that, the plant extracts must have acidic (ascorbic acid, citric acid and tartaric acid) and neutral (carbohydrate) constituents. The fluorescent properties of CDs are purely based on size, dopants, pH and solvents [13–15]. Doping of heteroatoms such as nitrogen, boron and sulphur on CDs, enhance the fluorescence and catalytic properties. Hence, the heteroatom doped CDs are mainly used as fluorescent probe in biological, catalytic and sensing applications [16–19].

Hylocereus undatus (H. undatus) is belonging to the Cactaceae family and native to South and Central America. H. undatus is a well-known plant for its fruit, and is commonly known as “dragon fruit”. It is fully covered by red skin with edible white flesh and commercially cultivated in Vietnam, Thailand, Malaysia, Israel as well as India and Sri Lanka [20–22]. The phytochemical screening of the H. undatus showed the presence of triterpenoids, alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, vitamin C, polyunsaturated fatty acids and lipids [23–25]. In the view of above, the fruit extract has been chosen as a carbon source for the preparation of CDs.

Methylene blue (MB) is a carcinogenic thiazine dye and it creates severe health problems such as mutations, cancer, skin irritations, allergic dermatitis and eye burns to both human and animals [26]. MB is widely used in surgery, microbiology, diagnostic fields and also in the aquaculture industry [27–29]. Since, MB is commonly found as pollutant in the effluents of these industries and hence the reduction of MB is environmentally and biologically important [30–31].

This work reports, the synthesis of nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) using H. undatus fruit extract and aqueous ammonia as a carbon and nitrogen precursor, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs are characterized by UV–Visible (UV–Vis), fluorescence spectroscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy techniques. Further, the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of N-CDs are tested on the L-929 (Lymphoblastoid-929) and MCF-7 (Michigan Cancer Foundation-7) cells. The catalytic activity of synthesized N-CDs are also analyzed towards the reduction of MB using sodium borohydride as reducing agent.

The H. undatus fruits (dragon fruits) were purchased from the local fruit shop in Dindigul, Tamilnadu, India. The Methylene blue (MB), aqueous ammonia, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (MTT) (98%) and sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) (99%) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. The L-929 and MCF-7 cells were purchased from the National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS), Pune, India. Double distilled water was used for the preparation of solutions throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of H. undatus Fruit Extract
ce:section.ce:para: The inner flesh of H. undatus fruit was carefully removed and cut into small pieces. An appropriate amount (50g) of H. undatus fruit was ground well using a mixer grinder. The obtained extract was filtered by cotton and Whatmann 40 filter paper. The obtained clear extract was used as a carbon source.

The synthesis of N-CDs from H. undatus extract was carried out by hydrothermal carbonization method. About 29mL of extract and 1mL of aqueous ammonia were mixed together and transferred to 50mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The mixture was kept at 180°C for 12h in a hot air oven. After the reaction, autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The resultant dark brown solution containing N-CDs was washed thoroughly using DMSO for the removal of unreacted organic moieties of an H. undatus extract. Further, the prepared N-CDs was filtered off using Whatmann 40 filter paper and then centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 30min to remove the large particles. Finally, the solution containing prepared N-CDs were collected, and kept at 4°C for the characterization and applications.

The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs were analyzed by UV–Visible (Perkin Elmer lambda) and Fluorescence (Jasco FP 8500) spectrophotometer. To find the maximum luminescence intensity, the excitation wavelength was varied from lower to higher wavelengths (280 to 360nm). The scan rate was set to 240nm/min and the slit width was fixed as 5nm. The HR-TEM images were acquired using JEOL JEM 2100 electron microscope (acceleration voltage of 200kV). A drop of N-CDs was coated onto a carbon coated Cu grid and dried at ambient temperature. The EDS and SAED pattern were also obtained using the same instrument. The FT-IR spectra of dried H. undatus extract and synthesized N-CDs were recorded by Jasco FT-IR 460 Plus spectrophotometer and the XRD analysis was carried out using PANalytical XPERT Diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54Å) at 40kV and 30mA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the Catalytic Activity of Synthesized N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The reduction reaction of MB was performed in a 3.5mL quartz cuvette with 1cm path length. Several experiments were employed to find out the optimum concentration of MB, NaBH4 and N-CDs (catalyst). The optimum concentrations of MB and N-CDs were determined and fixed. In the first reaction, 1mL of MB (1×10−4 M) was mixed with 0.20mL of freshly prepared ice-cooled NaBH4 (0.50M) and 1.80mL of water. In the second reaction, 10μL of prepared N-CDs was added to the above reaction mixtures. These reactions were continuously monitored at linear time interval using Perkin Elmer lambda UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The cytotoxicity of synthesized N-CDs was evaluated on L-929 and MCF-7 cells by 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (MTT) assay. These cells were seeded in a 96 well culture plate in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and incubated with 5% CO2 humidity at 37°C for 24h. After 24h incubation, the medium was removed and washed twice by PBS (10mM, phosphate buffer saline, pH7.4). The various concentrations of synthesized N-CDs such as 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50μL/mL were added to the cells and incubated for 24h at 37°C in serum free Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM). After the incubation period the medium was removed and the wells were washed with PBS solution. Then, 10μL of MTT solution (100μg/mL) was added to each well and these plates were further incubated for 4h at 37°C. Subsequently, the formazan (insoluble precipitate of MTT) in each well was dissolved using 100μL of 16% sodium dodecyl sulphate solution (solubilization solution). Identical cell culture procedure and conditions were used for the measurement of control experiments in the absence of N-CDs. The cell viability of L-929 and MCF-7 cells was analyzed by measuring the absorbance at 570nm using plate reading spectrometer. The cell viability was calculated by using the following formula;
(1)
Cell viability
%
=
Abs
s
Abs
c
× 100
where, Abs(s) and Abs(c) are the absorbance of sample with N-CDs and absorbance of control, respectively.

The fluorescent N-CDs were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of H. undatus fruit extract and aqueous ammonia at 180°C for 12h. The formation of CDs was confirmed by the color change of the fruit extract of H. undatus from light brown to blackish brown.

Fig. 1a shows the UV–Vis spectra of the carbon precursor (H. undatus fruit extract) and synthesized N-CDs in water. The H. undatus fruit extract is absorbed at 283nm in the UV region, while the N-CDs absorbed strongly at 269nm and 310nm which are corresponding to π–π* and n–π* transitions of the non-bonding electrons of carbonyl/amine functional group [32]. The absorption values of N-CDs spectrum changed notably due to the conversion of phenolic –OH to ammonium phenolate ions by the addition of aqueous ammonia [33]. The excitation spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1 (b)) shows two distinct peaks at 290 and 319nm, which corroborates the absorption spectrum.

Fig. 2 displays the fluorescence spectra of synthesized N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is gradually increased while increasing of excitation wavelength from 275nm to 320nm (Fig. 2a) and there is no prominent shift observed in the emission, which is probably due to the π* to π transitions of graphitic carbon cores. In addition, the emission peak is red shifted and the fluorescence intensity also decreased when moving the excitation from 320nm to 380nm (Fig. 2b). This type of excitation dependent emission property is common in all types of hetero atom doped CDs, which suggested that the emission property of synthesized N-CDs can be tuned to desired visible ranges. The excitation dependent emission spectra of N-CDs are arising from the π* to n transitions (radiative recombination) of carbonyl/amine functionalities in the surface [34–37]. The maximum emission intensity of 400nm is observed at excitation wavelength of 320nm. Therefore, the wide ranges of fluorescence spectra indicate the suitability of prepared N-CDs for biological imaging applications.

Fig. 3 presents the HR-TEM images, size distribution and SAED pattern of the prepared N-CDs. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 3a) shows that, most of the synthesized N-CDs are in spherical shape and uniformly distributed on the Cu grid. The resulting N-CDs have diameter ranging from 1.5nm to 5nm (Fig. 3b) with an average size of the synthesized N-CDs being 2.5 nm. In addition, the lattice fringes could be seen clearly in the high resolution TEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 3c) and this confirmed that the prepared N-CDs are graphitic in nature. The calculated d-spacing value (interlayer distance between the two parallel lattice fringes) is approximately 0.22nm and it is due to the presence of C (100) plane of the graphitic carbon [38,39]. In addition, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of N-CDs (Fig. 3d) displays diffused ring pattern, which implied that the amorphous phase of carbon [40].

The EDS spectrum is the complementary technique of HR-TEM, which provides clear information about the elements present in the N-CDs. Fig. 4 displays the EDS spectrum of prepared N-CDs. The EDS spectrum denotes the presence of C, N and O in the prepared N-CDS. The EDS spectrum of prepared N-CDs mainly consists of C (67.65%), N (5.82%) and O (26.53%), which clearly suggests that the nitrogen and oxygen atom is doped on the surface of the CDs [41].

Fig. 5 shows the FT-IR spectra of H. undatus extract and prepared N-CDs. The H. undatus extract shows absorption bands at 3420, 2924, 1712, 1197cm−1, which are corresponds to the stretching frequency of phenolic –OH, –C-H, –C=O and –C-O-C functional groups, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs shows the absorption bands at 3403, 2926, 1631 and 1114cm−1, revealed that the functional groups of H. undatus have been shifted and the nitrogen is present in the N-CDs. The –O-H stretching shifted from 3420 to 3403cm−1 for the prepared N-CDs due to the doping of nitrogen by amine functionalities and the –C-H functional group stretching vibration got shifted from 2924 to 2926cm−1 [33]. The prepared N-CDs has broad absorption band appearing at 1631cm−1 which belongs to the characteristic vibration of the C=C (sp2) stretching mode [42]. The small sharp absorption band at 1049cm−1appears from the stretching of –C-N groups in N-CDs [43]. In addition, the comparison between the –C=O and –C-O-C functional group intensities of H. undatus extract and N-CDs, showed that the intensities were decreased, shifted and broadened in prepared N-CDs due to the influence of carbonyl groups. Hence, the evidences from FT-IR spectra confirmed that the acid, amine, hydroxyl and carbonyl moieties present on the surface of synthesized N-CDs. Moreover, the phytoconstituents of H. undatus over the N-CDs surface result in the high water solubility and also suitable for optoelectronic and biological applications.

Fig. 6 displays the XRD pattern of synthesized N-CDs shows a very intense broad peak around 2θ=28.3° and a weak peak at 2θ=40.5° that are assigned to (002) and (001) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon in accordance with SAED pattern [44,45]. The d-spacing value for the N-CDs is calculated using Bragg's equation (Eq. (2)).
(2)
nλ = 2 d
sinθ
or
d =
nλ
2
sinθ
where, n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of incident X-rays (λ=1.54Å) and θ is the position of plane. The calculated d-spacing value of N-CDs is approximately 0.315nm and 0.224nm for C (002) and C (100) peaks, respectively.

Formation of N-CDs has been explained through polymerization, aromatization, nucleation and growth process [41]. In the first stage, the inter and intra molecular dehydration in the presence of H. undatus and liq.NH3 at above 100°C, may lead to the formation of bigger sized polymeric particles. Aromatization of bigger sized polymers will follow. At increasing temperature and time the aromatic clusters formed undergo the nucleation burst and this leads to the formation of N-CDs [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic Activity of N-CDs in the Reduction of Methylene Blue Using NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7a shows the UV–Vis spectra of the reduction of MB using NaBH4 in the absence of N-CDs catalyst for a time period of 2h. Pure MB showed the absorption maxima (λ max) value at 664nm [48]. After addition of NaBH4, the intensity of MB slightly decreased, but there is no obvious change observed while increasing of reaction time.

Fig. 7b shows the UV–Vis spectra of the reduction of MB using NaBH4 in the presence of N-CDs. After the addition of 10μL of N-CDs to the reaction mixture containing MB and NaBH4 the absorbance of MB is linearly decreased at regular time interval of 0.5min, which confirmed that the N-CDs act as a catalyst in the reduction of MB. The reaction completed at 6.5min. The degradation of MB by the N-CDs could be explained using Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism. The BH4¯ from NaBH4 and MB are simultaneously adsorbed on the surface of N-CDs. After adsorption, the BH4¯ ions transfer the electron to MB through N-CDs. Herein N-CDs act as electron transfer mediator, which reduce the energy barrier between the reactant and product [49–51]. In this reaction, large volume of H2 gas evolved. This excellent catalytic activity is attributed to the highly efficient electron-accepting capability of the prepared N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of In-vitro Cytotoxicity Using MTT Assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The in-vitro cytotoxicity of the synthesized N-CDs is examined on L-929 (Mouse fibroblast cell line) and MCF-7 (human breast adenocaricinoma cell line) by MTT assay. Fig. 8 to Fig. 11
shows the microscopic images of N-CDs (different concentrations) treated L-929 and MCF-7 cells and its % cytotoxicity. The results revealed that N-CDs exhibit anticancer potential towards MCF-7 without affecting the cellular viability. The images show that the L-929 normal cells are less affected by N-CDs than that of MCF-929 cancer cells, confirmed by counting the viable cells in both cell wells. In L-929 cells, the cell viability is>90% (Fig. 10) and in MCF-7 cells, the cell viability is<81% (Fig. 11) at higher concentration (50μL/mL) of N-CDs. The result suggested that, N-CDs are found to have no influence on the viability of L-929 cells (Fig. 8). On the other hand, the N-CDs exhibit higher cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells (Fig. 9). The prepared N-CDs caused more cell death in MCF-7 cells when compared to L-929 cells indicating target selective activity of the prepared N-CDs. It is quite possible that prepared N-CDs followed passive diffusion mechanism in cytotoxicity activity on L-929 cells and receptor-mediated endocytosis mechanism in anticancer activity on MCF-7 cells [52].

A simple, low cost, eco-friendly and one step hydrothermal carbonization method was employed for the synthesis of N-CDs from H. undatus extract as a carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as a nitrogen dopant. The green synthesized N-CDs have an average size of 2.5nm. The graphitic nature of the N-CDs was confirmed by XRD and SAED pattern studies. The calculated interlayer distance of the synthesized N-CDs was found to be approximately 0.22nm. The synthesized N-CDs emit intense bright blue color fluorescence under UV- light (at 365nm) when characterized by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. FT-IR and EDS results confirmed the doping of nitrogen over the surface of the N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs showed superior catalytic activity on the MB reduction and low cytotoxicity on L-929 and MCF-7 cells.
